findings.title: `test_empty_content` and `test_decoders_empty_cases` do not include zstd
findings.detail: Lines 149 and 160: both `@pytest.mark.parametrize` lists cover `b"deflate", b"gzip", b"br", b"identity"` but not `b"zstd"`. The `ZStandardDecoder.flush()` will raise `DecodingError` on an empty body (since `eof` will be False), so this is both a missing test and evidence of a real bug.

findings.title: flush() raises DecodingError on empty/no-data response for zstd
findings.detail: When no data has been received at all (e.g. a 204 No Content with `Content-Encoding: zstd`), `self.decompressor.eof` will be False (no stream has been started) and `flush()` will raise `DecodingError("Zstandard data is incomplete")`. Compare with `BrotliDecoder.flush()` which has an explicit `if not self.seen_data: return b""` guard. The existing `test_empty_content` test does not include `b"zstd"` in its parametrize list, which confirms this case is untested and broken.

findings.title: Multi-frame zstd decoding loop may be incorrect when data is chunked across frames
findings.detail: The loop `while self.decompressor.eof and self.decompressor.unused_data` re-creates a new decompressor for additional frames in the `unused_data`. However, if `decompress()` is called with data that only partially covers a second frame (i.e. the second frame isn't complete in this chunk), `self.decompressor.eof` will be False and `unused_data` will be empty. The remaining partial bytes are lost — they are neither stored nor handled. In practice, multi-frame zstd responses will rarely be encountered, but for responses streamed in small chunks, this will silently corrupt or truncate data. The correct approach (as done in urllib3) is to track remaining/unused bytes across `decode()` calls so that they are prepended to the next chunk.

findings.title: Incomplete error message in ZStandardDecoder.__init__
findings.detail: The ImportError message reads `"Using 'ZStandardDecoder', ..."`. The `...` truncation leaves out the explanation of the problem (the package is not installed or is too old). Compare with the BrotliDecoder message which is complete. This should say something like "Using 'ZStandardDecoder', but the 'zstandard' package (>= 0.18.0) is not installed."

findings.title: Missing conditional removal of 'zstd' from SUPPORTED_DECODERS when zstd is unavailable
findings.detail: After `SUPPORTED_DECODERS` is defined, there is a guard `if brotli is None: SUPPORTED_DECODERS.pop('br')`. There is no analogous `if zstd is False: SUPPORTED_DECODERS.pop('zstd')`. Without it, if the zstandard library is absent or too old, a request with `Content-Encoding: zstd` will attempt to instantiate `ZStandardDecoder`, which will raise an `ImportError` rather than gracefully falling back to identity decoding. The brotli pattern should be followed.

findings.title: `test_decoding_errors` does not include zstd
findings.detail: Line 167: `@pytest.mark.parametrize("header_value", (b"deflate", b"gzip", b"br"))` does not include `b"zstd"`. A test verifying that invalid zstd-encoded bytes raise `httpx.DecodingError` is missing.

findings.title: zstd sentinel value uses False instead of None for consistency
findings.detail: For brotli, the sentinel for 'not available' is `None`. For zstd it is `False`. This inconsistency means `_decoders.py` checks `if zstd is False` while brotli checks `if brotli is None`. The mixed convention increases cognitive load and could cause bugs if code like `if not zstd` is introduced later (since `None` and `False` both evaluate falsy, but `if zstd is None` would not catch `False`). Using `None` would be consistent with the brotli pattern and safer.

findings.title: `zstd` not added to `__all__`
findings.detail: `__all__` currently only exports `['brotli', 'set_minimum_tls_version_1_2']`. The new `zstd` symbol is imported in `_decoders.py` as `from ._compat import brotli, zstd`, so it works, but omitting it from `__all__` is inconsistent and could cause confusion about the public API of the module.

findings.title: Version check regex could fail if __version__ has no minor component
findings.detail: `re.search(r"^([0-9]+)\.([0-9]+)", zstd.__version__)` will return `None` if `__version__` somehow has no dot (e.g. `"1"`), causing a `NoneType has no attribute 'groups'` AttributeError on line 32. While unlikely for a real release, the `# type: ignore[union-attr]` suppresses the warning rather than handling it. A guard like `if m is None: zstd = False` would be safer. (urllib3 has the same pattern, so this is low priority.)
